text: fat is 55.5g. omega3 is where things start to fall off. for nuts, fat quality has to justify the calorie density.
carbs is 18.8g. fibre is doing a lot of the work here. for a nut, this is mostly about fat quality and support.
protein is 21.2g. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. protein is support here, not the whole argument.
vitamin e at 40% DV stand out most. that gives this food real vitamin support.
magnesium at 61% DV, zinc at 21% DV, iron at 20% DV stand out most. that gives this food real mineral support.

on the plus side. portable, practical snack food because nuts score real points in life because they are easy to use. fat and texture help satiety because they can hold up better than many snack foods. still a recognisable whole-food option because that keeps them cleaner than most processed snacks.

the drawbacks next. spread form makes overdoing it easier because still good, but less self-limiting than whole nuts. protein is useful but not elite because nuts help, but they are not protein stars. cost or allergy issues can limit use because practical downsides still count.
B tier. Best in small amounts where the fats and minerals matter more than sheer calories.
